what: Adaptive NSPLIT + full V dequant in stage1 + single mid buffer
why: Current fixed NSPLIT=16 wastes work for small kv_len (1024 tokens = only 64 per split). Reduce NSPLIT to 4 for kv<=1024, 8 for kv<=4096. Also dequant V fully in stage1 and store 512 bf16 values instead of 2x256 f32 (halves stage2 bandwidth). Eliminates interleave in stage2.

what: Hybrid MXFP4 K + FP8 V: use dot_scaled for Q*K^T with MXFP4, but load FP8 KV for V accumulation
why: V dequant from MXFP4 is expensive (branching fp4 decode per element). FP8 V is just load + multiply by scalar scale. 2x bandwidth vs MXFP4 but eliminates all dequant compute. Also avoids lo/hi split - direct 512-dim bf16 output.

what: BN=128 + num_warps=8 for stage1, reduce loop overhead
why: With BN=64 and kv=8192/NSPLIT=16, each split has 512 tokens = 8 iterations. BN=128 halves to 4 iterations, reducing loop overhead. num_warps=8 provides more parallelism for the larger BN blocks. FP8 V load of 128*512=64KB benefits from more warps.

what: Fine-tune NSPLIT=4 for kv<=1024, pre-allocate mid buffers, num_stages=3
why: At 0.8% gap to #1, need micro-optimizations. NSPLIT=4 for kv<=1024 reduces stage2 by 2x vs NSPLIT=8. Pre-allocate and cache mid buffers to avoid allocation overhead (significant for small batches). Try num_stages=3 for better pipelining of K load + score compute + V load.

what: Hybrid format selection: bf16 KV for small workloads, MXFP4+FP8 for large
why: For small workloads (bs*kv < threshold), bf16 avoids all dequant overhead and is simpler. For bs=4,kv=1024 only 4K tokens * 1152B = 4.5MB, well within bandwidth. For large workloads keep MXFP4 K + FP8 V which is bandwidth-optimal. The geomean benefits from improving both extremes.

what: Use bf16 KV cache for V instead of FP8, avoiding fp8 dequant and int64 offset overhead
why: FP8 V loads require int64 offset computation (expensive on AMD) and fp8->f32 conversion. bf16 V loads use native bf16 pointer arithmetic and no conversion. Trade 2x bandwidth for simpler load path. For small/medium batches, the latency savings from simpler loads may outweigh bandwidth cost.

what: Pre-scale Q, pre-contiguous fp8, eliminate int64 in V load
why: Micro-optimizations to v114: pre-multiply Q by sm_scale to avoid per-block multiply. Make fp8 V data contiguous with stride=V_DIM (not 576) to enable int32 offsets. Cache tensors across calls to avoid allocation.
override: Different from v116 (which changed NSPLIT/num_stages). This only changes Python-side prep and eliminates int64 in kernel.

what_failed: Closing gap from 19.19us to 19.04us (#1). Tried: tl.dot for V accum (+79%), num_stages=3 (+40%), 2-head-per-program (+66%), bf16 V (+100%). All regressed due to increased register pressure or bandwidth.
